summary: Skills have evolved in six months from a personal Claude Code feature into an open, cross-industry infrastructure standard adopted by Microsoft, Cursor, OpenAI, and others. This class examines four shifts that changed what skills are, then distills community-discovered design principles — trigger-focused descriptions, reasoning-based methodology bodies, explicit output contracts, and deterministic scripts — before applying them in a hands-on evaluation of real open-source skills.
objectives: Explain the four shifts that transformed skills from personal configuration tools into cross-platform, agent-called infrastructure
Write skill descriptions that act as reliable routing signals, including trigger phrases, output statements, and negative triggers
Apply the five requirements for an effective methodology body: reasoning, specified output, explicit edge cases, examples, and leanness
Decide when to delegate computations to deterministic scripts versus relying on the skill's probabilistic natural-language instructions
Evaluate real-world SKILL.md files for description quality, domain correctness, agent readiness, and composability
keyTakeaways: If a skill does not trigger, the cause is almost never the instructions — it is the description, which acts as the gatekeeper in progressive disclosure.
Because agents increasingly call skills without a human in the loop, descriptions must work as routing signals and outputs must be framed as contracts that downstream consumers can parse.
Skills compound in value over time, unlike ephemeral prompts: every edge case you discover and encode persists for all future invocations and for everyone who inherits the skill.
Effective methodology bodies provide reasoning and mental models rather than linear steps, so the agent can handle inputs the author never anticipated.
Keep the core SKILL.md lean (roughly 50-150 lines) and push examples, lookup tables, and reference material into Level 3 files that load on demand.
Operations that must be exact — unit conversions, CFL calculations, mesh metrics — belong in deterministic scripts, with the skill body providing the reasoning layer around them.

quiz.question: Your well-written, detailed skill never seems to activate when you ask the agent to do the task it covers. According to the notes, what is the most likely cause?

options.text: The methodology body is too long for the agent to hold in working memory
The description doesn't match the phrases users actually say, so the body is never loaded
The agent's context window is full, so it skips optional skills
The skill is missing an allowed-tools field in its frontmatter

quiz.explanation: As Poudel puts it, 'If your skill does not trigger, it is almost never the instructions. It is the description.' Under progressive disclosure, the agent only reads the frontmatter at startup — if the description doesn't match the request, the body might as well not exist.

quiz.question: Why does the shift from human-called to agent-called skills raise the stakes of skill design?

options.text: Agents invoke skills more slowly, so inefficient skills increase costs
Agents require skills to be rewritten in a structured format instead of Markdown
When an agent consumes subtly wrong output, the error propagates downstream with no human present to notice and correct it
Agents can only invoke one skill per session, so each invocation must succeed

quiz.explanation: When a human calls a skill, they can notice and adjust an imperfect result. In a multi-step agent pipeline, a subtly wrong output flows into the next skill or sub-agent's input with no recovery loop.

quiz.question: A skill instructs: 'Step 1: Check if Reynolds number exceeds 4000. If yes, use k-omega SST.' What design weakness does this illustrate?

options.text: A linear procedure with no reasoning — it breaks on inputs between categories, like Re = 3000 in the transitional regime
An output format that is too rigid for the agent to follow
Overuse of deterministic scripts where natural language would generalize better
A description that fails to include negative triggers

quiz.explanation: The notes contrast this brittle procedure with a reasoning-based version that explains the transition regime (2300 < Re < 4000) and why k-omega SST is recommended. Providing the mental model lets the agent make sensible judgments on cases the checklist doesn't cover.

quiz.question: Your skill needs to convert pressures from psi to Pascals and compute CFL numbers as part of its workflow. What approach do the notes recommend?

options.text: Write the conversion factors and formulas as explicit instructions in the SKILL.md body
Include several worked examples so the agent can pattern-match the arithmetic
Restrict the skill with allowed-tools so it cannot perform calculations at all
Put the calculations in deterministic scripts the skill calls, keeping the reasoning about when and how to use results in the SKILL.md

quiz.explanation: The notes state that behaviors that must be guaranteed — not probabilistically likely — belong in deterministic scripts in the scripts/ subdirectory, as in the materials-simulation-skills repository's cfl_checker.py. The skill body provides the reasoning layer; the script provides exact computation.

quiz.question: In the three-tier model for thinking about a skill's audience, what distinguishes a Tier 2 (methodology) skill from a Tier 1 (standard) skill?

options.text: Tier 2 skills are personal productivity tools that should stay on your own laptop
Tier 2 skills encode how experts perform high-value work — knowledge that would take a new student months to learn — while Tier 1 encodes 'we always do it this way' standards
Tier 2 skills are provisioned organization-wide by IT admins, while Tier 1 skills are project-specific
Tier 2 skills must be shorter than 150 lines, while Tier 1 skills have no length limit

quiz.explanation: Tier 1 covers shared standards like formatting rules and citation conventions; Tier 2 captures the intellectual methodology in practitioners' heads — the litmus test is what you'd want to communicate to a new graduate student that would otherwise take months to learn. (Personal workflows are Tier 3.)
